title: Variables and expressions
boardSubtitle: Aim with fixed inputs first, then use an assignment to store and reuse the portal side.
unlockSpotlightText:
A variable lets you store a value under a name.

New game action: before dropping the ball, write 'choose_input(...)' to choose which input the ball launches from.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What a variable is
unlockPrimerCards.body: A variable is a named place in the program where you can store a value and use it again later.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What assignment does
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'variable_name = 3' is an assignment statement. It stores the value 3 in a variable called 'variable_name'.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What you do here
unlockPrimerCards.body: To aim a ball you can use 'choose_input(3)', which launches the ball from the right input. If you already defined 'variable_name' as 3, you can put that variable name inside 'choose_input(...)' instead.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Drop a ball from input 1 (left)
practiceGoals.instruction:
Change the main program.
Add the line 'choose_input(1)' before 'drop_ball()' and run the program.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The code can look like this:
choose_input(1)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Drop a ball from input 3 (right)
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The code can look like this:
choose_input(3)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Drop the ball toward the portal
practiceGoals.instruction:
New board part: a 🟣portal has appeared on the pachinko board. If a ball enters it, something interesting happens.

Also notice the panel under the board called 'Board internals'. It shows information about the pachinko board that you can use.

It says that the variable 'portal_side' stores the input number where the portal is currently located.

Goal: write an assignment like 'target = portal_side', then use that variable inside 'choose_input(...)' and launch one ball toward the portal.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The important step in this goal is the assignment 'target = portal_side' before the launch:
target = portal_side
choose_input(target)
drop_ball()

primerCards.title: What the board gives you
primerCards.body: The board shows you which side the portal is on. Python reads that same side as 'portal_side'.

title: Conditionals
boardSubtitle: Read 'next_ball' before the launch and choose the right response.
unlockSpotlightText:
New game part: special balls.
From now on new ball types can drop.

There are 3 types:
🔴Negative ball (negative_ball) - this ball gives negative points
🟢Center ball (center_ball) - this ball gives bonus points if you drop it from input 2
🔵Portal ball (portal_ball) - this ball has a stronger effect if it goes into the portal

Each ball has its own synergy.
'Board internals' now shows which ball comes next. That value is stored in the variable 'next_ball'.
You can skip balls with 'skip_ball()'.
By using 'if', 'elif', and 'else', you can check 'next_ball' and react differently to different balls.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What 'if', 'elif', and 'else' do
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'if' checks the first condition. 'elif' lets you check another condition if the first one was false, and 'else' handles everything left over.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What the board gives you
unlockPrimerCards.body: The board shows the next ball before the launch. The same value is stored in the variable 'next_ball' in code.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What you do here
unlockPrimerCards.body:
1. Skip 'negative_ball'
2. Send 'portal_ball' to the portal
3. Send 'center_ball' to input 2

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Skip 'negative_ball'
practiceGoals.instruction:
The next ball is 'negative_ball'.
Use an 'if' statement to check it and skip it with 'skip_ball()'.
This goal is correct only if no ball launches. Press 'Run program' anyway, but do not add 'drop_ball()' after the 'if'. We add 'else' in the next goal.
practiceGoals.boardHint: 'skip_ball()' removes this bad ball from the queue. The run should still happen, but this goal should launch no balls.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Send the remaining balls toward the portal
practiceGoals.instruction:
Keep the rule for skipping the negative ball.
Add another branch for the safe balls. You can use 'else', or you can already start using 'elif' and 'else'.
Launch the remaining balls from the input above the portal.
practiceGoals.boardHint:
The negative-ball branch still skips the ball. The other branch should aim at the portal and launch it. A short version can look like this:

else:
    choose_input(portal_side)
    drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Launch 'center_ball' from input 2
practiceGoals.instruction:
In this situation the next ball is 'center_ball'.
Delete the current code and launch it from input 2.
practiceGoals.boardHint: This one-ball setup is already known to be a center ball, so a direct launch is enough.

title: Functions
boardSubtitle: Store one reusable tactic in the helper editor and call it from the main program.
unlockSpotlightText:
The helper editor is now unlocked. You can write code there too.
It makes sense to put a helper function there. Later you can call that helper from the main program. That lets you reuse code.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What a function is
unlockPrimerCards.body: A function is a named block of code that you can run again later.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What it does here
unlockPrimerCards.body: You can keep one helper function in the helper editor. You can run it from the main program with one command.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What you do here
unlockPrimerCards.body: For example, you can write a helper function that always launches balls from the input above the portal.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Call the helper
practiceGoals.instruction:
Call 'follow_portal()' once.
The helper reads 'portal_side' from the board, so you do not choose the side yourself.
practiceGoals.boardHint: The helper editor already stores the tactic. In the main editor you only need the call 'follow_portal()'.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Reuse the helper
practiceGoals.instruction:
Use the same helper again.
The portal is on the other side this time, but the helper still reads 'portal_side' dynamically, so the same call should still work.
practiceGoals.boardHint: If your helper already reads 'portal_side', you do not need to rewrite the helper body or the main call.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Write 'skip_negative()'
practiceGoals.instruction:
Edit the helper function.
Rename it to 'skip_negative()'.
It should check the next ball, and if that ball is 'negative_ball', it should skip it.
In this setup the visible next ball is negative, so a correct run should launch no balls. Still click 'Run program' so that bad ball gets removed from the queue.
Do not forget to call it from the main program under the new name.
practiceGoals.boardHint: This helper function should call 'skip_ball()' once. Do not choose an input and do not launch a ball here.

title: Loops
boardSubtitle: Use a loop to automate a short preview response.
unlockSpotlightText:
A loop repeats the code inside it several times. That means one loop can handle and launch multiple balls in a single run.
Inside that loop you can also use 'if', 'elif', and 'else' to react to different balls.
If you skip a ball, it is smart to use 'continue'. That stops the current loop step and starts the next one.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What a loop is
unlockPrimerCards.body: A loop repeats the code inside it several times, so you do not have to write the same lines again.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What it does here
unlockPrimerCards.body: One loop can handle several upcoming balls in a row and take the game one step closer to automation.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What to watch out for
unlockPrimerCards.body: If you use 'skip_ball()', that branch must end before the same loop step reaches another launch. 'continue' works well for that.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Repeat the helper at least three times
practiceGoals.instruction: Use 'for ... in range(3)' in the main program and put 'follow_portal()' inside the loop body so the helper launches at least three balls in one run.
practiceGoals.boardHint: Once this goal is complete, auto-run unlocks for short repeated sessions. 'range(3)' is the smallest loop that works here.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Repeat the helper at least four times
practiceGoals.instruction:
New game part: auto-run.
To the left of the 'Run program' button there is now an auto-run button. Try it: it runs the code for you at short intervals.

Increase the repeat count so the same helper launches at least four balls in one run.
practiceGoals.boardHint: Change the number inside 'range(...)'. 'range(4)' is the smallest loop that works here.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Handle every possible ball with a loop
practiceGoals.instruction:
Look at the 3 visible upcoming balls and handle them inside one loop.
On each repetition, check 'next_ball':
if it is 'portal_ball', call 'follow_portal()'
if it is 'negative_ball', skip it
if it is 'center_ball', send it to input 2.
practiceGoals.boardHint: A clear shape is: portal branch -> helper call, negative branch -> skip + continue, center branch -> choose_input(2) + drop_ball(). 'elif' helps keep those checks in one chain.

title: Lists
boardSubtitle: Read the 3 input multipliers, find the strongest one, and farm that input.
unlockSpotlightText:
New game part: each input now has its own multiplier.
'Board internals' and the pachinko board show these multipliers under inputs 1, 2, and 3.
Those multiplier values are stored in the list 'bonus_map'.

'bonus_map[0]' refers to the 1st item in that list (input 1)
'bonus_map[1]' refers to the 2nd item in that list (input 2)
'bonus_map[2]' refers to the 3rd item in that list (input 3)

The values in this list change on every program run.
Because the multiplier is very strong, your job is to drop balls from the input with the highest multiplier.

unlockPrimerCards.title: What the list stores
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'bonus_map' stores the three input multipliers in order.

unlockPrimerCards.title: How indexes match inputs
unlockPrimerCards.body: 'bonus_map[0]' matches input 1, 'bonus_map[1]' matches input 2, and 'bonus_map[2]' matches input 3.

unlockPrimerCards.title: How to search the map
unlockPrimerCards.body: With a loop you can go through 'bonus_map', inspect its values, and remember which input has the highest multiplier.

practiceGoals.title: Goal 1: Compare two multipliers
practiceGoals.instruction:
Compare the multipliers in 'bonus_map[0]' and 'bonus_map[2]'.
Store the index of the higher multiplier in the variable 'best_index' and then launch one ball from the matching input.
For the comparison you can use 'if bonus_map[0] < bonus_map[2]:'
practiceGoals.boardHint: 'bonus_map[2]' gives the multiplier value, not the input number. If the best slot is index 2, the matching input is 'best_index + 1', which means input 3.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_index = 0
if bonus_map[2] > bonus_map[0]:
    best_index = 2
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 2: Find the best multiplier out of all 3
practiceGoals.instruction: Turn the two-slot comparison into a real search. Use 'for multiplier in bonus_map:' to keep track of both the highest multiplier and its index, then launch one ball from that input.
practiceGoals.boardHint: Start with 'best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]', 'best_index = 0', and 'index = 0'. When the loop finds a bigger multiplier, store both the value and the index.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
practiceGoals.starterProgramSource:
best_index = 0
if bonus_map[2] > bonus_map[0]:
    best_index = 2
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()

practiceGoals.title: Goal 3: Use the best input
practiceGoals.instruction: Use the same best-input search and then drop at least 3 balls from that input with a loop.
practiceGoals.boardHint: Keep the search from Goal 2 and add a second loop that drops at least 3 balls from 'best_index + 1'. 'range(3)' is the smallest loop that works here.
practiceGoals.suggestedSnippet:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
for shot in range(3):
    choose_input(best_index + 1)
    drop_ball()
practiceGoals.starterProgramSource:
best_multiplier = bonus_map[0]
best_index = 0
index = 0
for multiplier in bonus_map:
    if multiplier > best_multiplier:
        best_multiplier = multiplier
        best_index = index
    index = index + 1
choose_input(best_index + 1)
drop_ball()
